fr:mainmatter.html:p: I am a PhD student at the University of Michigan as part of the Electrical and Computer Engineering program, where I work on reinforcement learning. My PhD advisor is Lei Ying. Previously, I obtained a BS from the University of Michigan in Mathematics and Computer Science, where I graduated with honors and high honors respectively. During my undergrad, I worked with Lei Ying as part of the SURE Program and as a teaching assitant for EECS 602. My full resume is also available here.

I am interested in continual reinforcement learning or more explicitly how (and what) to learn generalizable information online. With this, I am looking into {zero-shot, unsupervised, hierarchical} RL. I am also recreationally interested in Unix (see my dotfiles), HPC, and biologically-plausible learning algorithms. If you are also interested in any of these thing and want to chat, schedule a meeting!
If you are a student at University of Michigan and are interested in research, I am advising a few undergraduates with my advisor.

html:div.style: clear: both;

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is my forest (shoutout to Jon Sterling who is remarkably helpful). This website functions both as a personal knowledge management system, where I store my notes in notebooks, some accompanying solutions in the solution manuals, and any external resources I have made in /dev/null. Additionally, I store my thoughts in a variety of consumption mediums. Namely, both long-form and short-form notes / posts can be found in my blog. I also maintain two weekly posting obligations: a weeknotes / lifelog and a paper of the week.

html:ul.html:li: [September. 2024] I will officially be starting my PhD at University of Michigan
[August. 2024] I finished my undergraduate thesis and officially graduated!
[May. 2024] Our paper got accepted by Reinforcement Learning Conference
[January. 2024] I will be TAing EECS 602

fr:title.text: Meta Learning for Continual Reinforcement Learning: An Investigation

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Meta Learning for Continual Reinforcement Learning: An Investigation
Cost Aware Best Arm Identification
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @misc{kanariosCostAwareBest2024, title = {Cost {{Aware Best Arm Identification}}}, author = {Kanarios, Kellen and Zhang, Qining and Ying, Lei}, year = {2024}, doi = {10.48550/arXiv.2402.16710}, urldate = {2025-06-27}, number = {arXiv:2402.16710}, publisher = {arXiv}, file = {/home/kellen/Downloads/pdfs/storage/KCBBU7NQ/Kanarios et al. - 2024 - Cost Aware Best Arm Identification.pdf}, keywords = {Computer Science - Machine Learning,Statistics - Machine Learning}, langid = {english}, archiveprefix = {arXiv}, abstract = {In this paper, we study a best arm identification problem with dual objects. In addition to the classic reward, each arm is associated with a cost distribution and the goal is to identify the largest reward arm using the minimum expected cost. We call it Cost Aware Best Arm Identification (CABAI), which captures the separation of testing and implementation phases in product development pipelines and models the objective shift between phases, i.e., cost for testing and reward for implementation. We first derive a theoretical lower bound for CABAI and propose an algorithm called CTAS to match it asymptotically. To reduce the computation of CTAS, we further propose a simple algorithm called Chernoff Overlap (CO), based on a square-root rule, which we prove is optimal in simplified two-armed models and generalizes well in numerical experiments. Our results show that (i) ignoring the heterogeneous action cost results in sub-optimality in practice, and (ii) simple algorithms can deliver near-optimal performance over a wide range of problems.}, primaryclass = {cs}, eprint = {2402.16710}, month = {July} }

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is my blog, in which I write about a variety of topics including computer science, mathematics, and more, ranging from long-form deep dives to short, self-contained notes. See upcoming blogs and feel free to request! This blog also has a corresponding Atom feed.

Due to the unstructured nature of PhD life / knowledge work in general, I have been trying to come up with the most effective system to ensure I am Getting Things Done (ironically I found this system actually not that helpful).
Since Cal Newport is a professor (can relate to the knowledge work problem), I have been reading a lot of his stuff on productivity.

html:ul.html:li: Deep work.
Slow productivity.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I often refer back to Atomic Habits as it intuitively just makes so much sense to me.

html:ul.html:li: Start small: starting a habit should take less than 2 minutes.
Never miss a habit twice.
Habit anchors: I am always going to run so now after I run I stretch, which has resulted in remarkable stretching consistency (see my Weeknotes).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I have been using todoist and habitify. I decided on these tools primarily because of their API availability. I wanted to be able to add / complete / see outstanding tasks or habits for the day without the allure of everything else my browser has to offer. Also visualization for my Weeknotes.

html:ul.html:li: I had Claude write a little script to have my next tasks listed when clicked in my status bar, so I do not have to open the browser to check what I need to do today.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
It seems how to properly use these (todoist in particular) is the subject of much debate. Here, I will outline what I converged to / works for ME right now. I have broken down my system into a hierarchy:
Higher level: how to actually choose what to do.
Lower level: how to actually do the chosen task.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
This is effectively a task management system.
Work streams: I took this name from my investment banker friends (sounds cool / important). Basically, these are groupings I have made and allocated time for each day. They roughly consist of the following:
Research / work (4hrs first thing in the morning):
Active work towards new artifact or paper i.e. coding up ideas.
In the absence of ideas, studying existing relevant papers.
Deliberate practice (2hrs after run / lunch):
Name taken from Cal Newport.
As of writing this, a current example is spending ~2hrs a day writing / optimizing known kernels in CuTe / Pallas / NKI.
Basically, something I need to understand or master for research that is not novel.
Extraneous Study (30min - 1hr):
Things not directly related to research, but I am curious about.
I try to maintain study of related computer science fields. Primarily, studying a textbook in something like OS, computer architecture, etc.
Habits / daily upkeep (1hr normally end of day):
Mainly consists of reviewing Anki cards daily that come from extraneous study / deliberate practice.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
To decide on the next task, I use a priority-based system:
Things I will be working on today / until they are done. At most ONE thing per work stream.
The backlog of things to do next
An index to find things to move to priority 1 for the next day
Keep relatively small to avoid the crippling nature of choice - roughly on the order of things I want done this week.
A larger backlog of things - should be completed in the next month or so.
Things I am not working on right now, or have any need to be completed any time soon. This idea is mainly based on the MLFQ, which has been proven effective for balancing response / turnaround time. To maintain this system, I have reviews each day and week (tracking with habitify) to make sure I stay consistent.
Daily review:
For each work stream,
If I finished the allocated task for the day, get new task from priority 2.
Else, do nothing and continue with the current task.
Review inbox and move new tasks to corresponding work stream for review on Friday.
If immediately important, can move directly to priority 1 / 2.
Look at calendar for next day and timeblock work streams for the next day.
Since PhD work is so hard to predict, I have found timeblocking any further than one day in advance to be a futile effort.
Weekly review:
Typically done on Fridays, so that I can dynamically allocate time on weekends if needed.
For each work stream,
Look in remaining priority 2 and priority 3 tasks.
Define what I think a successful week would look like (e.g. if I get all these things done next week I will be happy).
Look at calendar for week to guide this decision.
Get rid of at least two of those tasks (initial plan always too ambitious).
Move / keep remaining tasks in priority 2.
Write Weeknotes for the week.

fr:title.text: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What to do when actually working on a task

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the harder part (and requires the most discipline). When working on a task, especially a more open-ended one like research, it is very common that a million other things come up that seem immediately important. All of the sudden, you have a great idea for the research project you haven't worked on in months.

It is crucial to collect and forget about these things until they are scheduled. This is the one thing I did take from Getting Things Done. The system should flow like water. Making this achievable is where the todoist API becomes crucial.
I have a script bound by dwm that pops up vim for me to write down the task and, on save, it sends it to my inbox in todoist.
I have more involved ones that allow me to actually allocate it to the specific work stream / priority if it is immediately obvious. Another thing that I have found useful to actually achieving this watery flow is the pomodoro technique. This basically lets me gaslight myself into thinking I can go to the other things as soon as the timer ends (which is intentionally always just close enough). By the end, I often no longer have this urge and can continue on the scheduled tasks.
This covers the math behind the online softmax algorithm required by Flash Attention (v1).
The first thing we must consider is numerical precision. Namely, the original softmax operation is given by
However, when operating with low precision data types (i.e. float16) the maximum value is 65536. Therefore, with \exp we can easily get numerically overflow.

To get around this, the online softmax algorithm adheres to the safe softmax. For safe softmax, we simply multiply the numerator and denominator by \exp (-m), where m = \max \{x_{1}, \ldots , x_n\}. This gives
Since x_i - m \leq 0, this ensures that each \exp (x_i - m) is bounded in a safe range.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Note that computing the \max over \mathbf {x} means that we must have all of the logits in a row of QK^T materialized in SMEM, or first loop over all of them to compute the max before computing the normalization constant.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The online softmax algorithm fuses the max reduction into the normalization pass, so a single sweep over the row suffices to compute the normalization with the max. The key difficulty is that the denominator depends on n for each i i.e.

Therefore, we must find a recursive relationship such that d_{i+1} = f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1}) for some function f. This would then allow us to locally compute d_{i+1} with only the local information of current x_{i+1} and the two running statistics d_i and m_i. This is exactly the kind of algorithm that is conducive to memory tiling.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. What is f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1})?
Answer. Consider d_{i+1} = d_i \cdot \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i + 1} - m_{i + 1}).

Proof. This can be seen by directly rewriting d_{i + 1}.
\begin {align*} d_{i + 1} &= \sum _{j = 1}^{i+1} \exp (x_j - m_{i + 1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i+1})\right ) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i})\right )\exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= d_{i} \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \end {align*}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The running denominator d is rescaled by e^{m - m_{\text {new}}} each time a new maximum is found, keeping it consistent with the safe-softmax shift.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Unfortunately, we cannot get around the second pass to compute the normalized output for \mathbf {p}. However, this is not the case in Flash Attention (v1).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Unlike in the online softmax, we are not interested in the outputted probability vector \mathbf {p}. Instead we are interested in the entire output matrix O given by
For more details see self-attention.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Notation. In correspondence with the notation in the online softmax, we define the following: x_{i,j} = \mathbf {q}_i \cdot \mathbf {k}_j, where \mathbf {q}_i is the ith row of Q and \mathbf {k}_j is the jth row of K. Similarly, \mathbf {o}_i is the ith row of the output matrix O given by \mathbf {o}_i = \sum _{j = 1}^{n} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_n)}{d_n} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now following the template provided by the online softmax, we must find a recurrence for \mathbf {o}_i. Namely, we will consider the sequence \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' given by \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \sum _{j = 1}^{k} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j, such that \mathbf {o}_{i,n}' = \mathbf {o}_n
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Claim. We have the following recurrence for \mathbf {o}_{i,k}':
\mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \mathbf {o}_{i, k-1} \cdot \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k

Proof. We will proceed by directly rewriting the recurrence for \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' as
\begin {align*} \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' &= \sum _{j = 1}^{k} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{k - 1} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_{k-1})}{d_{k-1}} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j\right ) \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,k} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k \\ &= \mathbf {o}_{i, k-1} \cdot \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,k} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k .\end {align*}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Thus, we are left with the following algorithm
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A key benefit of this algorithm is that it is entirely parallelizable across rows. This means that we can loop across entire tiles like in the GEMM case.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To understand the innovation of the backward pass in flash attention, we must first find all of the gradients we must compute.
We will compute each gradient individually.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. \mathbf {P} and d\mathbf {P} are N \times N matrices. Materializing this is equivalent to materialing the attention matrix in the forward pass.

Insight. recomputing \mathbf {P}_{ij} and d\mathbf {P}_{ij}.
First, we can easily recompute \mathbf {P}_{ij} with \mathbf {Q} and \mathbf {K} (which are both N \times d and must be resident in memory) via \mathbf {P}_{ij} = \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}}
We can apply the same trick to d\mathbf {P}_{ij} with d\mathbf {O} and \mathbf {V} (both N \times d). Namely, d\mathbf {P}_{ij} = \sum _{k} d\mathbf {O}_{ik} \mathbf {V}_{jk} = d\mathbf {O}_{i}^T \mathbf {V}_j
Never need an N \times N matrix.
Problem. \mathbf {D} requires computing the global dot product \mathbf {P}_i \cdot d\mathbf {P}_i.

Insight.
To get around this, we precompute \mathbf {D} using the \mathbf {O} values from the forward pass and the current d\mathbf {O} values. In particular,
\begin {align*} \mathbf {D}_i &= \mathbf {P}_{i} \cdot d\mathbf {P}_{i} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {P}_{ij} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= \sum _{j} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \left (\frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j}\right ) \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {O}_{i} .\end {align*}
Thus, we can compute \mathbf {D}_i directly from the vectors d\mathbf {O}_i, \mathbf {O}_i.

html:ul.html:li: In the forward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute and store \mathbf {O} values in memory.
Compute and store \mathbf {L} values in memory

html:ul.html:li: Between the forward and backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {O}. (This is done automatically in JAX)
Use \mathbf {O} and d\mathbf {O} to compute \mathbf {D}. O(n) memory.

html:ul.html:li: In the backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {V}_j via \mathbf {Q}, \mathbf {K}, d\mathbf {O}. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {Q}_i via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {K}_j via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This will also serve as a bit of notes from A Primer on Memory Consistency and Cache Coherence
Syntactical things I need to remember from Murphi docs. Most things are straightforward, but there are a few things to note:
Declarations. A declaration is a variable or a collection of variables that are used in the rules.
Rules. A rule is a transition from one state to another

html:ul.html:li: Each rule can optionally have an initial condition required to be fired
We can easily define rules for all the processors or collection of n things with ruleset.
For our use case, rules are used to define

html:ol.html:li: Controller actions
Receiving a request from the network
Retrying stalled requests

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Startstates. A startstate is the intial configuration for all our declarations.

html:ul.html:li: Must assign a value to every global variable

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Invariants. An invariant is something that Murphi checks to ensure is true at all possible configurations of the system.
Overview straight from the docs. Repeat forever:

html:ol.html:li: Find all rules whose conditions are true in the current state. (i.e. conditional expressions are true, given the current values of the global variables).
Choose one arbitrarily and execute the action, yielding a new state.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The first thing we need to do is understand all the things that can go awry.
First in the valid-invalid protocol example we were given they only have two virtual channels. However, we need 3 channels to handle, request messages, forwarded messages, and response messages.
Let us briefly understand why this is the case. Detecting a deadlock is equivalent to detecting a cycle in a dependency graph, so it is sufficient to show a cyclic dependency.
If we issue a GetS this goes to the directory, giving us an edge N1 -> D.
Then the directory issues a Fwd-GetS to the ownder, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2
The owner then needs to send data to the requester, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2 -> N1.
Namely, the data must go through the same channel as all of the requests depending on it.
To avoid this, we partition into 3 channels:
Request: GetS, GetM, PutM
Forwarded: Fwd-GetS, Fwd-GetM, PutAck, Inv
Response: InvAck, Data
We directly enumerate these in our MessageType as MessageType: enum { GetS, -- request for shared copy GetM, -- request for modified (exclusive) copy PutS, -- evict shared copy PutM, -- evict modified copy (w/ data)
FwdGetS, -- forwarded GetS to owner FwdGetM, -- forwarded GetM to owner Inv, -- invalidation request
Data, -- data response InvAck, -- invalidation acknowledgment PutAck -- put acknowledgment }; Cache controller. We will start with MSI cache controller, given by the following table:
Notably, we are missing some of the messages in the figures below. We can handle these based on the AckCount ProcState: Record state: enum { P_Invalid, P_Shared, P_Modified, --stable states PT_IS_D, --sent GetS, waiting for Data PT_IS_DI, --sent GetS, got Inv, waiting for Data then go to I PT_IM_AD, --sent GetM (from I), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_SM_AD, --sent GetM (from S), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_IM_A, --got Data for GetM, waiting for remaining InvAcks PT_MI_A, --sent PutM, waiting for PutAck PT_SI_A, --sent PutS, waiting for PutAck PT_II_A --waiting for PutAck, already invalidated }; val: Value; ack: AckCount; -- tracks InvAck count End;
Remark. The load, store, and replacement all only alter the states of the coherence protocol for the permanent states. Therefore, to simulate the system it is sufficient to only implement the rules that initiate these state transitions i.e. evict in modified state or load from invalid state.
Directory controller. Now for the directory controller:
Remark. We actually end up adding a second transient state to the directory controller M^D. This is to handle the PutM, GetM race described below.
Weird Race Conditions
Problem. When implementing the PutM rule to downgrade from modified to invalid. We must NOT get rid of the data until we have been acknowledged, so that we can still service forward requests. This is a transition to MI^A. However, we can still receive the PutAck causing us to transition to I before servicing the Fwd-GetM request. This is lost data.
Solution. We must add a M^D state to the directory controller. In this state, if we get a PutM request from a node that is not our current owner we stall and wait for data to arrive. For M^D to transition to M, we must send the data back to the directory even though we are doing a three-hop, so that the directory knows the data has also been forwarded to the new owner. It is unclear to me the best way to do this without this state.
Problem.
The above solution causes a deadlock, where two processors both store miss and one load misses. The "winning processor" of the store will be the second one. The first one will get the data from the directory transitioning from IM^{AD} \to IM^{A}, but the second one will then trigger a Fwd-GetM overwriting the first.
The load miss transitions to IS^D. However, when it receives an Inv it stalls according to the above figure. This blocks the Fwd-GetM from reaching the other winning processor because they are on the same VC. Thus, we have a deadlock where the owner never gets the data.
Solution. We need to add another transient state not included in the figure. Namely, we add II^{A} that says we were in IS^{A}, but we got invalidated and now we are waiting for our PutAck before we transition to I.
Remark. When the home directory is in M and we get a GetS we cannot clear owner as directed by figure. Otherwise in S^D, we will not be able to tell if the PutM is from the owner and stall, so we only remove the owner once we have received the data.

Aside from the modifications discussed above, the rest was straightforward and we now are ready to move on to MESI.
Cache controller. We will start with MESI cache controller, given by the following table:
Design choice 1. Since we allow immediate upgrade to M, without forwarding, if E upgrades to M, then the value in the directory sent to the next GetS / GetM would not have updated value. It seems we must either
not allow E to silently upgrade to M and then we do not need forwarding, or
allow E to silently upgrade to M and treat E as M basically and forward everything. Notably, neither of these have to write back on PutE, but we will go with 1 because that is how it is indicated in the figure.
Design Choice 2. In order to indicate that the directory received the first GetS we introduce a ExclusiveData message. Upon receiving, ExclusiveData in IS^D we transition to E.
Remark. Once we have sent the PutE, or the PutM EI^A and MI^A are functionally the same. I include both for completeness but verified that transitioning directly to MI^A from E works the same.
Directory controller. We start with the analagous table.
Similar to the cache controller, we also add a E^D state for completion that can be absorbed into M^D introduced in the MSI protocol.
Weird Races.
Problem. First processor does GetS and is in IS^D but the Home directory sends it exclusive data and is now in E. Then second processor does GetM and Home issues Fwd-GetM to processor one, which arrives before data.
Solution. Need to stall on Fwd-GetM and Fwd-GetS in IS^D until data arrives
Problem. An even crazier race happens when we get invalidated before the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS arrive. Due to our previous design decision, we transition to II^A, where if we receive a PutAck we transition to invalid. This then loses the needed forward data.
Example. The timeline:
Proc_1 performs a load miss → IS^D
Home receives first GetS from Proc_1 → E and sends ExclusiveData to Proc_1
Proc_2 issues GetM → IM^AD
Home sends Inv to Proc_1
Home forwards Fwd-GetM to Proc_1
Proc_1 receives Inv → II^D
Proc_1 receives ExclusiveData → I Data is lost
Solution. We once again have to use the ExclusiveData message. We can then see that we got ExclusiveData in II^D and transition to E instead of I. E will then correctly handle the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS.
We are left with the following state diagrams for the cache and directory controllers respectively

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Rapidly exploring random trees (RRT) is a very simple, elegant and widely used algorithm for motion planning. The algorithm proceeds as follows:

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Algorithm. For k = 1 to K do
x_{rand} \gets \mathsf {RANDOM\_STATE}()
x_{near} \gets \mathsf {NEAREST\_NEIGHBOR}(x_{rand}, \mathcal {T})
u \gets \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT}(x_{near})
x_{new} \gets \mathsf {NEW\_STATE}(x_{near}, u, \Delta t)
\mathcal {T}.add\_vertex(x_{new})
\mathcal {T}.add\_edge((x_{near}, u, x_{new})) return \mathcal {T}

Remark. Given some dynamics model \dot {x} = f(x, u), \mathsf {NEW\_STATE} is some approximate integration, such as Euler integration i.e. x_{new} = x_{near} + f(x_{near}, u) \Delta t Preferable to use higher-order method like Runge-Kutta.

Remark. \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT} is determined by whether the system is holonomic or non-holomonic. If the agent is holonomic, then u will just be the control corresponding to the difference in the two states.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As I grow more interested in abandoning batched updates and the replay buffer, the importance of elibility traces grows with me. Due to this, I thought it finally time to re-prove / understand what makes these traces so eligible.
The reference followed is Sutton and Barto (Ch 12.).
Notes on the paper Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need.

For,
b batch dimension,
n sequence length,
d hidden dimension,
h number of heads. The total FLOPS for attention are roughly bnd^2. This comes from
3nd^2 FLOPS to compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V.
Need to do this b times for each input in batch. and the total memory accesses are roughly \underbrace {bnd}_{X} + \underbrace {bhn^2}_{\text {softmax}} + \underbrace {d^2}_{\text {projection}}. This gives high arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {h}{d} + \frac {1}{bn}\right )^{-1}\right )

However, if we do incremental inference then we must multiply our total number of memory accesses by n i.e. bn^2d + nd^2. This gives arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {n}{d} + \frac {1}{b}\right )^{-1}\right ), which requires large batch and short sequence length.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. I believe we just ignore the softmax memory contribution in the inference case because it does not scale with n anymore as we are computing the logits just for the next token. Therefore, it becomes a \frac {d}{h} term, which we can safely ignore?
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key is that the \frac {n}{d} term comes from the bn^2d term, where we are loading h (b \times n \times d / h) K and V matrices n times. Thus, we can improve the \frac {n}{d} term by a factor of h by simply not using a different K and V matrix for each head. This is the entire idea behind MQA.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Similar to the KV-cache, we still can use h Q matrices because we do not need to load n of them into memory because only the last one matters for inference.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Group query attention is the same idea as MQA but instead of using one K and V for every head, they use a group of them. Where obviously the number of groups needs to be less than the number of heads.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Grouped query uses subset of K and V matrices.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also inspired by this idea of reducing the dependence of K and V on the sequence length is sliding window attention.

The idea behind sliding window attention is to ensure the number of weight matrices needed for the keys K and values V does not scale with the sequence length. Intuitively, this means allowing each word to "attend" to only some fixed number of previous words rather than the whole sequence

html:figure.html:figcaption: "the" only sees "on" and "sat" rather than the full sentence.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Note that the gradient is with respect to \theta , where \theta is just the third argument to \mathsf {ELBO} as defined above. Notably, \theta _t will show up elsewhere later but will NOT be backpropagated through.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The above motivates the expectation step of the EM algorithm. Namely how can we compute the lower bound expectation for the best possible q.

Recall (or review) from the discussion on ELBO that q(z) = p(z | x; \theta ) maximizes the ELBO for a given x and \theta . Therefore, we actually want to learn q_{\phi }(z, x, \theta ) \approx p(z | x; \theta ). To do so, we can simply maximize the ELBO (duh). Formally, \phi _{t+1} = \argmax _{\phi } \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q}\left [\log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta _t)}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta _t)}\right ]
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Now this is all cool theoretically but how TF do we actually maximize this thing?
Answer. It is highly dependent on the parametrization of p and q.

html:div.html:p:
We define the problem as
p(z) \sim N(0, 1)
p(x | z; \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\theta }(z), \sigma _{\theta }(z))
q_{\phi }(z | x, \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _\phi (x)) where \mu _{\{\phi , \theta \}}, \sigma _{\{\phi , \theta \}} are parametrized neural networks. Then we can rewrite the ELBO as (ignoring constants)
\begin {align*} \mathsf {ELBO}(x, q, \theta ) &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [\log p(Z) - \log \frac {p(x \mid Z; \theta )}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta )}\right ] \\ &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [-\frac {Z^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((Z - \mu _{\theta }(Z))^2 - (Z - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] .\end {align*}
We face one more obstacle.
html:div.html:div: Question. We need to sample realization z \sim q_{\phi }(\cdot |x, \theta ). So isn't our realization z a function of \phi . How would we optimize this?

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
For q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _{\phi }(x)). The reparametrization trick allows us to differentate through a random sample by sampling from q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) via z(\phi ) = \mu _{\phi }(x) + \sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon , where \epsilon \sim N(0, 1). Then, for a given sample \epsilon , we can easily compute 
abla _{\phi } z(\phi ) = 
abla _{\phi } \mu _{\phi }(x) + 
abla _{\phi }\sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon

html:div.html:p: Now for a given sample x_i \sim \mathcal {D}, we can get a sample z_i(\phi ) \sim q(\cdot \mid x_i, \theta ) that is differentiable with respect to \phi . Using this, we can get the stochastic gradient estimate \nabla _q \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\phi } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ]

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Finally, we have reached the maximization step. This step is fairly straightforward after all the progress we have made. Once we have a tight lower bound i.e. q(z) \approx p(z \mid x; \theta ) then we simply fix q and maximize the ELBO for \theta i.e. in the above example and algorithm we instead compute \nabla _\theta \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\theta } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] Alternating between these steps yields the expectation-maximization algorithm the ML oldheads mythologize.

Setup: We are given a joint distribution p(X, Z; \theta ), where X and Z are random variables with their joint parametrized by \theta . We are also given a dataset \mathcal {D} containing realizations of only X i.e. Z is a latent random variable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to learn p(x; \theta ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, p(x; \theta ) = \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z = \int p(x | z; \theta )p(z) \mathrm {d}z, which is computationally intractable for most continuous Z.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Can we update \theta for a given realization of x and z?
Answer. Yes!!!
fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Motivation: Suppose we had some q(z) that we could sample realizations of Z from. Then we want to find some reasonable \ell _{\theta }, such that \theta ^* \approx \arg \max _{\theta } \mathbb {E}_{x, \sim p(x; \theta ^*), z \sim q(z)}[\ell _{\theta }(X, Z)]
Remark. q(z) can potentially (and will) depend on the sample x_i. This is OK because we are given x_i's, which we can then just pass to q to get the corresponding z_i.
Why is this the case? Because then (for well-behaved \ell _{\theta }) \theta _{t + 1} = \theta _{t} - \alpha \nabla _{\theta _{t}}\ell _{\theta _{t}}(x_i, z_i) \to \theta ^* i.e. SGD is a reasonable thing to do, where the gradient is for a single sample of x_i and z_i which we have readily available from our dataset \mathcal {D} and our distribution q that we can sample from. This idea comes up a lot in optimization, such as the \log trick in the original policy gradient.

Similar to the \log trick, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) comes from a simple multiply and divide trick. Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \frac {q(z)}{q(z)} \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &\geq \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} .\end {align*} The last inequality follows directly from Jensen's Inequality. However, note that this is only a lower bound and therefore not exactly what we want. For example, if we were to pick q \sim N(0, 1) and then optimize the ELBO for \theta we would not necessarily find a good \theta for the likelihood \log p(x; \theta ). Luckily, we can actually get the difference \log p(x; \theta ) - ELBO in closed form! Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathrm {ELBO} &= \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &= \int \left (\log p(x; \theta ) - \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(x, z; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )p(x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \mathrm {KL}\big (q(z) \mid \mid p(z | x; \theta )\big ) .\end {align*} Thus, to tighten the lower bound we can optimize q_{\phi }(z) to try to match p(z | x; \theta ). As a sanity check, lets make sure these match when q(z) = p(z \mid x; \theta ). \begin {align*} \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} &= \int \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log \frac {p(x; \theta )p(z \mid x; \theta )}{p(z \mid x; \theta )}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log p(x; \theta )\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log (x; \theta ) .\end {align*} You may be wondering how we use this algorithmically to find \theta ^*? This will be the main idea of expectation maximization. Feel free to check it out!

I have been using LazyVim for some time now, but I have now run into issues multiple times where understanding how LazyVim is doing something is far more difficult than if I had written my own setup. I allocated one day for this adventure and really just wanted to make sure I had support for \TeX , python, forester, and C/C++. Due to my (self-imposed) time constraint, I do not have the associated resources linked for each of the things discussed below. At some point, I hope to come back and more thoroughly cover each of the components.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: To my surprise, a lot of the features that I had come to take for granted were actually options set up internally by Lazyvim. For example, I was shocked with 8 space indents!! and I could not even copy from one terminal instance to another... Due to this, I went and found all of the options I liked from Lazyvim and added them to my new configuration in configs/options.lua.
For this, we will be using the defacto standard lazy.nvim. This is actually straightforward and kind of "just works". Just follow the installation guide in their documentation.

This is one of the main motivations for me making the switch. It seems nvim-cmp has finally been replaced with a new blink.cmp, so that is what we will be using.
Kellen Kanarios202534Rebuilding My (Neo)Vim Config From Scratch › Language Server Protocol It turns out there is a lot that goes into getting LSP setup correctly.
First we must actually install the language servers. To do this the easiest way, we use the mason.nvim and mason.nvim-lspconfig plugins. At some point, I might actually figure out how to set up lsp myself without lspconfig but that point is not now.
Through nvim-lspconfig, we can set up each of the servers we want to have LSP support. I just set up clangd, pyright, and texlab. This was a bit ridiculous. The first of many challenges was around import resolution in python. To remedy this, I needed to write a function to find the virtual environment directory and then set the pythonPath to the venv python binary. Previously, I think I was just using pylsp and installing it as a pip package to each python venv. I much prefer the new way, and I think pyright is overall a much better lsp.
Kellen Kanarios202534Rebuilding My (Neo)Vim Config From Scratch › Forester Completion Another necessary completion source for me is the one provided by forester.nvim. Similar to vimtex, the reference completion support is VERY useful. Obviously, I need completion when I am writing this blog!!! This was a little more involved. The first difficulty was that the completion source provided by the forester.nvim plugin was for nvim-cmp. It turns out this is a prevalent enough problem that the author of blink.cmp wrote an additional plugin blink.compat to allow for nvim-cmp completion sources. While this sounds all fine and good, nvim-compat expects plugins that return the completion source themselves, whereas in forester.nvim the completion source is just one submodule of a more feature-rich plugin. To get around this, I needed to look into the blink.compat code and find how they are registering the sources and just do it myself.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Going all the way back to the Gilles Castel blog post, I have always been partial to snippets that auto-expand. I had them set up prior to Lazyvim but with Lazyvim I had resigned to using friendly-snippets with native nvim snippets. Since I was already redoing everything, this time around I decided not to compromise. Once upon a time (right when it came out I think?) I tried out Luasnips, but it seems that they now have far more extensive features. They are also natively supported by blink.cmp!! It feels necessary that I plug the awesome repo that ports the original Ultisnips snippets to Luasnip. With this, I was able to easily add my own forester snippets!!!

A fun little thing that I had been hoping to do for awhile and is finally now possible - I can load latex snippets when inside math environments in forester!!!! To do this, I looked into the forester treesitter grammar and found the corresponding nodetypes for math envs. It was then straightforward to detect whether we were in a math env and to load the associated latex snippets.

There is a very cool add-on to anki called AnkiConnect that has an associated plugin anki.nvim. Basically, AnkiConnects allows you to make requests to Anki and receive/send useful information from/to your decks. Unfortunately, anki.nvim built-in commands didn't seem all that useful to me. However, it provided the necessary infrastructure for me to accomplish my desired workflow.

Namely, I made my own command that queries Anki for the deck names, which you can then pick from using telescope pickers. When you select one it will create a new flashcard in a specified flashcard directory under a directory created based on the deck name. You can then send this card to that deck using the existing AnkiSend command.

I also continued the snippet fun here. When writing Anki cards, you can write latex code between [latex] [/latex] delimiters. I wrote a quick function to detect whether we are in these delimiters and if we are then to load the latex snippets from the previous section. I also added some basic anki filetype plugins to insert things like these delimiters.

I often hear this question come up amongst performance-driven developers, and for the longest time I could never get a straight answer as to why the stack was so much better? After a long foray into the inner workings of how memory allocation is actually done, I think I finally have an answer. I do not want to forget this, so I am doing a quick write up here.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
This all comes down to what happens during each memory allocation. Prior to actually running the program, the operating system allocates some amount of memory for both the stack and the heap (I think around 2-4MB?). When you allocate memory on the stack i.e. int x = 1;
Stack just increments stack pointer.
Writes value to new address pointed to by stack pointer. When you allocate memory on the heap i.e. int *x = malloc(sizeof(int)), we must instead choose how best to fulfill the request, which is a whole research direction in itself.
One such implementation is the buddy algorithm However, malloc itself is not a system call and therefore is not terribly slow. A real issue occurs when we run out of pre-allocated heap memory. This requires invoking a system call like sbrk to increment the size of the allocated memory for the heap.
If no available pages, OS must evict and write to disk...
Formally, this is known as "heap growth". Generally, if you know how much memory you need it is good practice to just allocated all up front to minimize any potential system calls. With this, the performance difference is not too drastic :)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The 15 most recently modified trees, by git commit time. Generated by scripts/recent — do not hand-edit.

Trivially, we can do character-based tokenization i.e. mapping each character to a unique number. For example, \mathsf {chr}(97) = \text {``a''} and \mathsf {ord}(\text {``a''}) = 97. The compression ratio will be > 1 because each token corresponds to a character potentially consisting of more than one byte.

html:ol.html:li: Very large vocabulary
Many characters are quite rare.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. There is a fundamental tradeoff between compression ratio and vocabulary size. As an example, suppose we map every sequence of n bytes to a token. This would achieve a compression ratio of n. However, this would require 256^n tokens in our vocabulary.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Problem 2 was not entirely straight-forward to me. This actually reminds me of an idea from my information theory course, where we can assume we are given a "prior" over human language then we want to map low-probability characters to large strings in order to minimize the expected length (see huffman coding).

In byte-based tokenization, each byte is a token achieving a compression ratio of exactly 1, where a byte \mapsto [0, 256] . This is very elegant in the sense that our vocabulary size is fixed at 256.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. However, with this, the length of our encoding is the number of bytes in the string, which will become too long.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In word-based tokenization, we simply split the string into the corresponding words then map each of these to a unique integer.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. The vocabulary size is "unbounded" i.e. if we see a word we have not seen before then we need to extend the vocabulary size.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For the byte pair encoding (BPE), we instead train the tokenizer on raw text to automatically determine the vocabulary.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. In GPT2, they first split the raw text into words and then do this byte pair encoding.

Remark. There are a few additional implementation details to consider for project 1.
\mathsf {encode} loops over all merges. Only loop over merges that matter.
Detect and preserve special tokens (e.g. <|endoftext|>)
Use pre-tokenization (GPT2 regex)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To understand the innovation of the backward pass in flash attention, we must first find all of the gradients we must compute.
We will compute each gradient individually.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. \mathbf {P} and d\mathbf {P} are N \times N matrices. Materializing this is equivalent to materialing the attention matrix in the forward pass.

Insight. recomputing \mathbf {P}_{ij} and d\mathbf {P}_{ij}.
First, we can easily recompute \mathbf {P}_{ij} with \mathbf {Q} and \mathbf {K} (which are both N \times d and must be resident in memory) via \mathbf {P}_{ij} = \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}}
We can apply the same trick to d\mathbf {P}_{ij} with d\mathbf {O} and \mathbf {V} (both N \times d). Namely, d\mathbf {P}_{ij} = \sum _{k} d\mathbf {O}_{ik} \mathbf {V}_{jk} = d\mathbf {O}_{i}^T \mathbf {V}_j
Never need an N \times N matrix.
Problem. \mathbf {D} requires computing the global dot product \mathbf {P}_i \cdot d\mathbf {P}_i.

Insight.
To get around this, we precompute \mathbf {D} using the \mathbf {O} values from the forward pass and the current d\mathbf {O} values. In particular,
\begin {align*} \mathbf {D}_i &= \mathbf {P}_{i} \cdot d\mathbf {P}_{i} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {P}_{ij} \\ &= \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= \sum _{j} d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \left (\frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j}\right ) \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \sum _{j} \frac {\exp \left (\mathbf {Q}_{i} \cdot \mathbf {K}_{j}\right )}{\mathbf {L}_{i}} \cdot \mathbf {V}_{j} \\ &= d\mathbf {O}_{i}^{T} \mathbf {O}_{i} .\end {align*}
Thus, we can compute \mathbf {D}_i directly from the vectors d\mathbf {O}_i, \mathbf {O}_i.

html:ul.html:li: In the forward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute and store \mathbf {O} values in memory.
Compute and store \mathbf {L} values in memory

html:ul.html:li: Between the forward and backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {O}. (This is done automatically in JAX)
Use \mathbf {O} and d\mathbf {O} to compute \mathbf {D}. O(n) memory.

html:ul.html:li: In the backward pass,

html:ol.html:li: Compute d\mathbf {V}_j via \mathbf {Q}, \mathbf {K}, d\mathbf {O}. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {Q}_i via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.
Compute d\mathbf {K}_j via \mathbf {Q}_i, \mathbf {K}_j, \mathbf {L}_i, \mathbf {D}_i. O(d) memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Setup: We are given a joint distribution p(X, Z; \theta ), where X and Z are random variables with their joint parametrized by \theta . We are also given a dataset \mathcal {D} containing realizations of only X i.e. Z is a latent random variable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to learn p(x; \theta ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, p(x; \theta ) = \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z = \int p(x | z; \theta )p(z) \mathrm {d}z, which is computationally intractable for most continuous Z.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Can we update \theta for a given realization of x and z?
Answer. Yes!!!
fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Motivation: Suppose we had some q(z) that we could sample realizations of Z from. Then we want to find some reasonable \ell _{\theta }, such that \theta ^* \approx \arg \max _{\theta } \mathbb {E}_{x, \sim p(x; \theta ^*), z \sim q(z)}[\ell _{\theta }(X, Z)]
Remark. q(z) can potentially (and will) depend on the sample x_i. This is OK because we are given x_i's, which we can then just pass to q to get the corresponding z_i.
Why is this the case? Because then (for well-behaved \ell _{\theta }) \theta _{t + 1} = \theta _{t} - \alpha \nabla _{\theta _{t}}\ell _{\theta _{t}}(x_i, z_i) \to \theta ^* i.e. SGD is a reasonable thing to do, where the gradient is for a single sample of x_i and z_i which we have readily available from our dataset \mathcal {D} and our distribution q that we can sample from. This idea comes up a lot in optimization, such as the \log trick in the original policy gradient.

Similar to the \log trick, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) comes from a simple multiply and divide trick. Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \frac {q(z)}{q(z)} \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &\geq \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} .\end {align*} The last inequality follows directly from Jensen's Inequality. However, note that this is only a lower bound and therefore not exactly what we want. For example, if we were to pick q \sim N(0, 1) and then optimize the ELBO for \theta we would not necessarily find a good \theta for the likelihood \log p(x; \theta ). Luckily, we can actually get the difference \log p(x; \theta ) - ELBO in closed form! Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathrm {ELBO} &= \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &= \int \left (\log p(x; \theta ) - \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(x, z; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )p(x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \mathrm {KL}\big (q(z) \mid \mid p(z | x; \theta )\big ) .\end {align*} Thus, to tighten the lower bound we can optimize q_{\phi }(z) to try to match p(z | x; \theta ). As a sanity check, lets make sure these match when q(z) = p(z \mid x; \theta ). \begin {align*} \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} &= \int \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log \frac {p(x; \theta )p(z \mid x; \theta )}{p(z \mid x; \theta )}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log p(x; \theta )\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log (x; \theta ) .\end {align*} You may be wondering how we use this algorithmically to find \theta ^*? This will be the main idea of expectation maximization. Feel free to check it out!
Rapidly exploring random trees (RRT) is a very simple, elegant and widely used algorithm for motion planning. The algorithm proceeds as follows:

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Algorithm. For k = 1 to K do
x_{rand} \gets \mathsf {RANDOM\_STATE}()
x_{near} \gets \mathsf {NEAREST\_NEIGHBOR}(x_{rand}, \mathcal {T})
u \gets \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT}(x_{near})
x_{new} \gets \mathsf {NEW\_STATE}(x_{near}, u, \Delta t)
\mathcal {T}.add\_vertex(x_{new})
\mathcal {T}.add\_edge((x_{near}, u, x_{new})) return \mathcal {T}

Remark. Given some dynamics model \dot {x} = f(x, u), \mathsf {NEW\_STATE} is some approximate integration, such as Euler integration i.e. x_{new} = x_{near} + f(x_{near}, u) \Delta t Preferable to use higher-order method like Runge-Kutta.

Remark. \mathsf {SELECT\_INPUT} is determined by whether the system is holonomic or non-holomonic. If the agent is holonomic, then u will just be the control corresponding to the difference in the two states.

Remark. Note that the gradient is with respect to \theta , where \theta is just the third argument to \mathsf {ELBO} as defined above. Notably, \theta _t will show up elsewhere later but will NOT be backpropagated through.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The above motivates the expectation step of the EM algorithm. Namely how can we compute the lower bound expectation for the best possible q.

Recall (or review) from the discussion on ELBO that q(z) = p(z | x; \theta ) maximizes the ELBO for a given x and \theta . Therefore, we actually want to learn q_{\phi }(z, x, \theta ) \approx p(z | x; \theta ). To do so, we can simply maximize the ELBO (duh). Formally, \phi _{t+1} = \argmax _{\phi } \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q}\left [\log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta _t)}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta _t)}\right ]
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Now this is all cool theoretically but how TF do we actually maximize this thing?
Answer. It is highly dependent on the parametrization of p and q.

html:div.html:p:
We define the problem as
p(z) \sim N(0, 1)
p(x | z; \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\theta }(z), \sigma _{\theta }(z))
q_{\phi }(z | x, \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _\phi (x)) where \mu _{\{\phi , \theta \}}, \sigma _{\{\phi , \theta \}} are parametrized neural networks. Then we can rewrite the ELBO as (ignoring constants)
\begin {align*} \mathsf {ELBO}(x, q, \theta ) &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [\log p(Z) - \log \frac {p(x \mid Z; \theta )}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta )}\right ] \\ &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [-\frac {Z^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((Z - \mu _{\theta }(Z))^2 - (Z - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] .\end {align*}
We face one more obstacle.
html:div.html:div: Question. We need to sample realization z \sim q_{\phi }(\cdot |x, \theta ). So isn't our realization z a function of \phi . How would we optimize this?

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
For q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _{\phi }(x)). The reparametrization trick allows us to differentate through a random sample by sampling from q_{\phi }(\cdot | x) via z(\phi ) = \mu _{\phi }(x) + \sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon , where \epsilon \sim N(0, 1). Then, for a given sample \epsilon , we can easily compute 
abla _{\phi } z(\phi ) = 
abla _{\phi } \mu _{\phi }(x) + 
abla _{\phi }\sigma _{\phi }(x) \cdot \epsilon

html:div.html:p: Now for a given sample x_i \sim \mathcal {D}, we can get a sample z_i(\phi ) \sim q(\cdot \mid x_i, \theta ) that is differentiable with respect to \phi . Using this, we can get the stochastic gradient estimate \nabla _q \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\phi } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ]

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Finally, we have reached the maximization step. This step is fairly straightforward after all the progress we have made. Once we have a tight lower bound i.e. q(z) \approx p(z \mid x; \theta ) then we simply fix q and maximize the ELBO for \theta i.e. in the above example and algorithm we instead compute \nabla _\theta \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\theta } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] Alternating between these steps yields the expectation-maximization algorithm the ML oldheads mythologize.

The OS lets user code run directly on the CPU for speed, but with hardware-enforced limits that prevent a buggy or malicious program from compromising the rest of the system. Two questions motivate the mechanism:

html:li.html:em: How do we make sure a program doesn't do what we don't want it to do?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first question is answered by hardware User mode vs kernel mode enforced via the Trap table, with a controlled Syscall mechanism as the only path from one to the other. The second is answered by periodic timer interrupts that trap to the OS, which performs a Context switch and runs the scheduler.
The whole stack is bootstrapped during the Boot sequence, when the kernel installs the trap table and starts the timer before handing control to user space.

Two privilege levels supported by the CPU. User mode restricts the running process to a limited set of instructions and a limited view of memory — usually just its own address space. Kernel mode runs the OS with full access to all instructions and all of physical memory.

Switching between modes is mediated by hardware via a Trap table populated at boot. The OS sets up the table; the CPU enforces it. A process cannot promote itself to kernel mode arbitrarily — it must invoke a Syscall mechanism, which traps to a fixed entry point in the OS.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Kernel mode is dangerous because the address space is effectively the entire machine. The mode bit + the trap table together are what makes it safe to time-share a CPU between mutually distrusting programs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A hardware-readable table installed by the OS at boot that maps each kind of exceptional control-flow event (syscall, interrupt, page fault, illegal instruction, ...) to the address of an OS handler. When the CPU takes a trap, it consults this table to decide where to jump, switches into kernel mode, and runs the handler.

The table is what makes it safe to expose privileged operations to user mode: user code never gets to pick the destination of a mode-switch, only the trap number. The OS controls every entry point, the CPU enforces the indirection.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Installing the trap table is itself a privileged operation. It happens once, early in boot, before any user process exists.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A syscall is a user-mode-initiated request for a privileged service from the kernel. Because user code cannot jump into kernel code directly, the mechanism is built around a single trap instruction plus a pre-agreed register convention identifying which service to perform.
The end-to-end sequence:

html:ol.html:li: User code places the syscall number in a pre-defined register and invokes the trap instruction.
Hardware saves enough state to resume the process and switches to kernel mode.
Hardware jumps to the OS-installed handler via the Trap table.
The handler reads the syscall number from the agreed register and dispatches to the corresponding OS routine.
The OS validates the request, performs the work, and arranges return values per the calling convention.
The OS restores user state and returns the process to user mode.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Because the register convention is fixed by ABI, the user-mode side of a syscall is typically hand-written in assembly within libc.
Remark. Interrupts work via a similar trap-handler mechanism, but are initiated by hardware (timer, device) rather than user code.
Remark. The kernel must validate any pointers or arguments the user supplies — user code cannot be trusted not to ask the kernel to read kernel memory on its behalf.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The operation of saving the full execution state of one process (or thread) and restoring another's so the CPU can resume it as if it had never been interrupted. On a trap into the kernel, the hardware stashes the user-visible state (PC, condition codes, stack pointer) into a known location, and the OS saves the remaining general-purpose registers as part of the switching code.
Two flavors of state are involved:

html:ol.html:li: User registers: the current program's context (general-purpose registers, stack vars).
Kernel registers: meta-information used to resume the trap correctly (PC at trap, syscall number, etc.).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Context switches are not free — they incur the cost of cache and TLB pollution in addition to the direct cost of saving/restoring state. This is the cost amortized by larger scheduling quanta and a recurring concern for any Concurrency system.
The fixed series of steps a machine performs from power-on until a usable operating system is running. Each stage runs from a known location and hands off control to the next.

html:ol.html:li: Power signal resets the CPU to a known state.
The CPU fetches from a hard-wired address — the BIOS (or UEFI firmware). The BIOS runs basic hardware checks (POST).
The BIOS loads and jumps to the bootloader (e.g. GRUB) from a known location on disk.
The bootloader places the kernel image at the correct address and jumps to its entry point.
The kernel performs early setup: installs the Trap table (a special privileged instruction), starts the timer interrupt, sets up memory management, and finally enters user space.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware is hard-wired only to find the BIOS. Everything past that is software the user can replace, which is why dual-booting and custom bootloaders work.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An example of how to tile a matrix multiplication, our tile partitions the output matrix M \times N by block sizes BS_N and BS_M as seen in the image below (drawn using excalidraw)
The block size of k BS_K is then basically the accumulation axis, where we loop over k if we cannot fit the whole row / column of A and B into shared memory.

In pytorch, tensors are contiguous blocks of memory, where their shape is controlled by strides. A stride basically just refers to how far you have to jump in physical memory to correspond to incrementing the logical index i.e. in a (2, 3) matrix each row requires jumping 3 spots in physical memory. Thus, stride[1] = 3.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Calling .view((m, n)) just changes the stride but does not modify the physical memory. If you call .view that results in a non-contiguous view, then you cannot call .view again because it assumes contiguous indexing to properly adjust the strides. To deal with this, they have .contiguous() and .reshape() is basically .contiguous().view().
Remark. All elementwise operations make a copy i.e. x + y. A useful function for masked attention is triu().

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Einops is a useful python library to keep track of high-dimensional matrix operations i.e. matmuls over batch_size, seqlen, etc. High level overview
Initialize like X[float.tensor, "batch seq hidden"] = torch.tensor((2, 3, 4))
Can perform matmul with named dims like z = einsum(x, y, "batch seq1 hidden, batch seq2 hidden -> batch seq1 seq2")
reduce: x = reduce(x, "x y z -> x y 1") is just like mean(x, axis=-1)
rearrange: x = rearrange(x, "... (heads hidden1) -> ... heads hidden 1")

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backlink accumulator for all things kernel DSLs.

Worked homework for Three Easy Pieces. One section per chapter; the conceptual material for each chapter lives in the corresponding concept pages — Processes Intro across Process, Process API, Process control block; Process Interlude across fork(), wait(), exec(), Unix signals.

html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads does SJF scheduling deliver the same turnaround as FIFO scheduling?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. For workloads that arrive in increasing order of total job time.
html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads and quantum lengths does SJF deliver the same response times as Round-robin scheduling?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. For shorter jobs and longer quantum lengths, RR and SJF should deliver similar response times.
html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with SJF as job lengths increase?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Response time degrades because the longest jobs have to wait for each of the shorter jobs to finish. If these shorter jobs become longer than the response time grows linearly.
html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with RR as quantum lengths increase? Can you write an equation that gives the worst-case response time, given N jobs?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. The response time is the average over stride length number of preceding jobs. The formula can be written as \mathrm {RT} = \frac {1}{N} \sum _{i = 1}^{N} (i - 1)S = \frac {S(N - 1) N}{N} = S(N - 1)
html:ol.html:li:
Run process.py with the flags -l 5:100, 5:100. What should the CPU utilization be?
Answer. The flags correspond to running two processes each with 5 instructions where each instruction has probability one of using the CPU. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu with this, we can conclude that the utilization is 100%. We will execute one instruction for the first process until it completes, then the five instructions of the next process. If both are waiting on IO, then I do not think this would be the case?
Solution. Using the solution flags -cp, there is 100% utilization.

Run process.py with the flags -l 4:100, 1:0. How long does it take to complete both processes?
Answer. The flags correspond to running one process with 4 instructions with each instruction having probability one of using the CPU and one process with one instruction that uses the IO. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 io io_done It will take 4 + \text {time of IO}. Since the process with 4 non-IO-dependent instructions will run each of those instructions and then the next process will begin which must wait for the entire duration of IO.
Solution. Using the -cp flags it takes 11 timesteps to complete which is \approx 4 + \text {time of IO}.

Now switch the order of the processes: ./process-run.py -l 1:0,4:100. What happens now? Does switching the order mat- ter? Why?
Answer. The output of the code is Process 0 io io_done
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu Depending on the time of IO (the -L flag), it should take \max (5, 1 + \text {time IO}). Switching the order does matter because we can run the CPU instructions while waiting for non-blocking IO. This motivates the idea of scheduling.
Solution. It only takes 7 timesteps because we are able to perform the IO operations off the CPU as expected.

We'll now explore some of the other flags. One important flag is -S, which determines how the system reacts when a process issues an I/O. With the flag set to SWITCH_ON_END, the system will NOT switch to another process while one is doing I/O, instead waiting until the process is completely finished. What happens when you run the following two processes, one doing I/O and the other doing CPU work? (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH_ON_END)
Answer. The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED READY 1 3 BLOCKED READY 1 4 BLOCKED READY 1 5 BLOCKED READY 1 6 BLOCKED READY 1 7* RUN:io_done READY 1 8 DONE RUN:cpu 1 9 DONE RUN:cpu 1 10 DONE RUN:cpu 1 11 DONE RUN:cpu 1 As expected, it runs the IO process, blocking the CPU process until it is completed.

Now, run the same processes, but with the switching behavior set to switch to another process whenever one is WAITING for I/O (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH ON IO). What happens now? Use -c and -p to confirm that you are right
html:ol.html:div: Answer. I assume that at timestep 1 we will still RUN:io. However, we will then switch the PID: 1 for the next 4 timesteps until it is completed. After one additional BLOCKED timestep, we will run RUN:io_done and complete both processes.

Solution. The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 3 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 4 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 5 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 6 BLOCKED DONE 1 7* RUN:io_done DONE 1
html:ol.html:li:
One other important behavior is what to do when an I/O completes. With -I IO RUN LATER, when an I/O completes, the process that issued it is not necessarily run right away; rather, whatever was running at the time keeps running. What happens when you run this combination of processes? (./process-run.py -l 3:0,5:100,5:100,5:100 -S SWITCH ON IO -I IO RUN LATER -c -p) Are system resources being effectively utilized?
Answer. The resources are not efficiently utilized because we end up waiting until all of our CPU-bound processes finish before returning to the IO-bound process, which calls another IO:run and we do not get any concurrency benefit.
Solution. The code output is pretty long but the end output is Stats: Total Time 31 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (67.74%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (48.39%)

Now run the same processes, but with -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE set, which immediately runs the process that issued the I/O. How does this behavior differ? Why might running a process that just completed an I/O again be a good idea?
Answer. The behavior differs by returning to the IO-bound process, which can then immediately call the next IO:run before returning access to the CPU-bound processes, improving concurrency. In an attempt to predict the next IO call, it might be a good idea to run the process that just completed IO because it is more likely to make another IO call.
Solution. The corresponding statistics are Stats: Total Time 21 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (100.00%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (71.43%)

Now run with some randomly generated processes, e.g., -s 1 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 2 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 3 -l 3:50,3:50. See if you can predict how the trace will turn out. What happens when you use -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE vs. -I IO RUN LATER? What hap- pens when you use -S SWITCH ON IO vs. -S SWITCH ON END?
Answer. Since each instruction has a 50% chance of being CPU or I/O, I do not think there will be much benefit to IO_RUN_LATER vs. IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE. However, I believe it will be crucial to enable -S SWITCH_ON_IO over -S SWITCH_ON_END as the number of processes grow because we should always have waiting CPU instructions to run. In the case of two processes, we still may not improve that much because they will likely both be IO-bound at some point.
Solution. For -s 1 (aka the seed), the observed performance between IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE and IO_RUN_LATER is the same. However, we do observe an improvement on both IO and CPU usage when we enable SWITCH_ON_IO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Deliberate-Practice (6/66).

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork(). Before calling fork(), have the main process access a variable (e.g., x) and set its value to something (e.g., 100). What value is the variable in the child process? What happens to the variable when both the child and parent change the value of x
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Both the parent and the child get a copy of the variable values i.e. x. When they modify x it only affects the copy of x in their local address space.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that opens a file (with the open() system call) and then calls fork() to create a new process. Can both the child and parent access the file descriptor returned by open()? What happens when they are writing to the file concurrently, i.e., at the same time?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Both do have access to the same file descriptor. I believe that they can interleave writes if writing at the same time but for me whichever process got ran first always finished writing prior the other starting. This may be a scheduling thing, where one process is able to complete before the other is scheduled?
html:ol.html:li: Write another program using fork(). The child process should print "hello"; the parent process should print "goodbye". You should try to ensure that the child process always prints first; can you do this without calling wait() in the parent?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Using the previous problem, in the child, we can write something to a file. Then, in the parent, we can enforce an ordering by making the parent wait in a while loop until it reads the value written by the child. However, this is terribly inconvenient (and probably why wait() exists).
html:ol.html:li: Now write a program that uses wait() to wait for the child process to finish in the parent. What does wait() return? What happens if you use wait() in the child?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. wait() returns the pid of the child process that died. Using wait() in the child just immediately returns -1.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork() and then calls some form of exec() to run the program /bin/ls. See if you can try all of the variants of exec(), including execl(), execle(), execlp(), execv(), execvp(), and execvP(). Why do you think there are so many variants of the same basic call?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. It seems that some of them support different types of arguments i.e. execv family allows variable size argument list. Additionally, there is a distinction in the environment. This seems to be a list of environment variable values that exec{l,v}e() will search over. The p family use the same search as the shell i.e. the PATH.
html:ol.html:li: Write a slight modification of the previous program, this time using waitpid() instead of wait(). When would waitpid() be useful?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. If a parent process has multiple children, then wait() will only wait until the first one dies. waitpid() lets you specify waiting on a specific child process.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates a child process, and then in the child closes standard output (STDOUT_FILENO). What happens if the child calls printf() to print some output after closing the descriptor?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Nothing prints in the child process. However, stdout is still open in the parent process and able to print.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates two children, and connects the standard output of one to the standard input of the other, using the pipe() system call
html:ol.html:div: Answer. This required fflush() for me. I am not sure if this is true in general?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The process abstraction, the syscalls for working with processes on Unix, the hardware-software contract that makes time-sharing safe, and the scheduling policies that decide who runs when.
A running program, plus all the state the OS needs to pause it and resume it later. A process consists of:

html:ol.html:li: Memory: an Address space holding the program's code, stack, and heap.
Registers: general-purpose registers carrying current computation, plus special-purpose registers:

html:ul.html:li: Program counter (PC): the next instruction to run.
Stack pointer and frame pointer: where the process sits on its stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A process moves between three states:

html:ul.html:li: running: currently on a CPU.
ready: runnable, waiting for the Scheduling to give it a CPU.
blocked: waiting on I/O or another event, not runnable.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads (see Concurrency Intro) share an address space within a process but each have their own PC, register set, and stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The minimum operations any OS must expose for working with Processes:

html:ol.html:li: Create: load a program from disk, set up its address space (stack, heap, code), and jump to main. The OS also initializes argc/argv.
Destroy: kill a misbehaving or unwanted process.
Wait: block until another process completes — useful for ordering dependent work.
Miscellaneous control: e.g. suspend / resume.
Status: report on running processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The stack is allocated up front. The heap starts small and grows via malloc, which calls into the OS (brk/sbrk) when it runs out of headroom.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: On Unix, the concrete realization of this API uses fork(), wait(), and exec() — covered separately.

The kernel-side data structure holding all per-process state. There is one PCB per Process, threaded together into the process list. The PCB is what the OS reads and writes during a Context switch: the saved register set lives here, as does the process state, the open files, the parent pointer, and so on.
An example from xv6:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads need an analogous thread control block; see Concurrency Intro.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Unix system call that duplicates the calling Process. After fork() returns, two processes exist where there was one. The child gets its own copy of:

html:ol.html:li: Address space.
General-purpose registers.
Program counter — the child returns from fork() at the same instruction the parent does.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two processes are distinguished by fork()'s return value:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Which process is scheduled first after fork() is non-deterministic. Use wait() if you need ordering.
Remark. The copy is logical, not physical: real implementations use Copy-on-write so the duplicated address space only allocates fresh pages when one side writes to them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Blocks the calling Process until one of its children terminates. The OS marks the child's PCB ZOMBIE on exit, keeping enough state (exit status, resource usage) for the parent to retrieve via wait(); only when the parent collects it is the child fully reaped.
wait() returns the PID of the child that died. Variant waitpid(pid, ...) blocks on a specific child — useful when a parent has multiple children and only one is interesting.
Used to enforce ordering between parent and child code paths after fork(), or to know when an exec()'d program finishes.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Calling wait() with no children to wait on returns -1.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replaces the calling Process's program with a new executable. exec() does not return on success — the original code, data, stack, and heap are discarded and the new program runs in their place. The PID is unchanged.
What exec() does internally:

html:ol.html:li: Loads the new executable's code and static data, overwriting the current.
Re-initializes stack and heap.
Jumps to the new program's entry point with arguments passed as argv.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The canonical Unix pattern is fork() then exec() — the shell uses this to launch a command without becoming that command:
Redirection is implemented by opening a file in the child between fork() and exec(), attached to STDOUT_FILENO:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The many variants — execl, execle, execlp, execv, execvp, execvP — differ in how they take arguments (list vs. vector), whether they use the PATH environment variable, and whether they take an explicit environment.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An asynchronous notification delivered to a Process. A process sends a signal with kill(pid, signum) (the name is misleading — most signals don't kill); the recipient can install a handler with signal(signum, handler) to "catch" it, otherwise the default action runs (often termination).

Signals are the OS's mechanism for events the recipient didn't ask to be told about: SIGINT from Ctrl-C, SIGSEGV from a Page fault on an invalid page, SIGCHLD when a child exits, SIGTERM from an administrative kill.
Who can signal whom depends on the user owning the process — a user can signal their own processes; the superuser can signal any process.

The OS lets user code run directly on the CPU for speed, but with hardware-enforced limits that prevent a buggy or malicious program from compromising the rest of the system. Two questions motivate the mechanism:

html:li.html:em: How do we make sure a program doesn't do what we don't want it to do?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first question is answered by hardware User mode vs kernel mode enforced via the Trap table, with a controlled Syscall mechanism as the only path from one to the other.
